title: Generation of an anomalous linearly dispersing spin-polarized band in Bi-based topological insulators
authors: Matthias Kronseder, Thomas Mayer, Jan Minár, Magdalena Marganska, Hedwig Werner, Florian Schmid, Rebeca Diaz-Pardo, Ivana Vobornik, Jun Fuji, Cornelia Streeck, Alexander Gottwald, Hendrik Kaser, Bernd Kästner, Christian H. Back
abstract: We report the generation of an anomalous linearly dispersing, spin-polarized band in Bi-based topological insulator (TI) thin films, induced by soft Ar-ion bombardment followed by annealing. This extra band -- which we call the anomalous linearly dispersing state (ALS) -- is superimposed on the regular band structure including the topological surface state (TSS), spans an unusually large energetic range of up to ${\sim}\,\SI{650}{\milli\electronvolt}$ at the $Γ$-point, and appears near the Fermi energy. Spin-resolved measurements indicate spin-momentum locking with a helicity \emph{opposite} to that of the regular TSS. The Fermi velocity of the ALS, $v_\mathrm{F} = (5.1\pm 0.4)\times 10^{5}\,\frac m s$, is indistinguishable from that of the regular TSS, $(5.3\pm 0.5)\times 10^{5}\,\frac m s$. The observation is reproducible across samples of varying thickness and was confirmed at two independent synchrotron radiation facilities. We discuss different mechanisms for the physical origin of the observed ALS including sputtering-induced TSS relocation, bi-layer formation by,e.g., chalcogen removal, and high-index surface relocation.
summary: This paper reports the experimental generation of a new, highly energetic, and spin-polarized electronic band in Bi-based topological insulators using ion bombardment and annealing. This 'anomalous' state is unique because it possesses a spin helicity opposite to the standard topological surface state and spans an unusually large energy range. The study provides a detailed investigation into the possible physical origins of this state, such as surface reconstruction or chemical changes.
main_problem: The emergence of an unexpected, highly energetic, and spin-polarized electronic band (the ALS) in Bi-based topological insulators following surface treatment via ion bombardment and annealing.
main_result: The discovery of an 'anomaline linearly dispersing state' (ALS) that coexists with the regular topological surface state, possesses an opposite spin helicity, and spans a large energy range of ~650 meV with a Fermi velocity nearly identical to the regular TSS.
method: The researchers used a 'sputter-heating cycle' (Ar-ion bombardment followed by annealing) on BSTS/Bi2Se3 heterostructures and characterized the electronic and structural changes using Spin-resolved ARPES, XPS, and AFM.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of (Bi1-xSbx)2(Te1-ySey)3 (BSTS) / Bi2Se3 heterostructures grown on SrTiO3(111) substrates, subjected to surface modification via soft Ar-ion sputtering and annealing.
key_observables: Electronic band dispersion, spin-momentum locking (helicity), Fermi velocity (vF), energy range of the ALS, surface roughness, and chemical stoichiometry.
important_parameters: ALS energy range (~650 meV), Fermi velocity (approx. 5.1-5.3 x 10^5 m/s), and sputtering voltage (250-500 V).
main_assumptions: The authors evaluate several models (Rashba 2DEG, TSS relocation, Bi-bilayer formation, etc.) but note that no single current model fully accounts for all observed properties.
figures_summary: Figure S1 shows the evolution of ARPES scans through different treatment stages: thermal decapping, sputtering (blurred scans), and annealing (improved contrast with the emergence of the ALS feature).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of defect-induced states in TIs, describes the experimental generation of the ALS via surface treatment, presents the characterization of the new band's dispersion and spin properties, discusses the surface morphology changes, and evaluates various physical mechanisms for the origin of the ALS.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a) ARPES scan with Eph = 36eV and EF set to 0eV from a sample with (2)BS/(27)BSTS after a sputtering- heating cycle. The scan shows a faint TSS band structure in the background around -0.2eV. The main feature is, how- ever, the linearly dispersing band starting at around -0.65eV (ALS), and the extra features close to EF. b) The sister sam- ple with nominally the same stoichiometry and thickness was treated similarly and shows also the linearly dispersing bands spanning this time only an energetic range of 0.55eV, while the extra features close to EF is not seen. c) same plot as in a) but highlighting the different features: i) ALS, ii) ordinary TSS, iii) extra bands close to EF. d)...

FIG. 2. a-c) ARPES scans of samples with (2)BS and different thicknesses of the BSTS layer after sputtering-heating cycles: a) for a dBSTS = 6QL, b) 8QL and c) 27QL. For all plots the onset of the Dirac cone is highlighted by a dashed cone with the same size in all plots. In d) spin-polarization scans for two different in-plane wave vectors k∥= ±0.06˚A−1 is plotted, k∥< 0 in green, k∥> 0 in orange for the sample with dBSTS = 27QL. The same data is also overlaid in c) at the corresponding wavevectors and energies (marked with green and orange arrows) while the polarization in % is given by the colorbar in c).

FIG. 3. a) and b) show typical AFM scans directly after the MBE growth of samples with (2)BS and different thicknesses of the BSTS, a) for dBSTS = 4QL and b) dBSTS = 31QL. After the beamtime and hence after the sputter-heating cycles the roughness and especially the morphology changed, c) and d). c) shows the surface structure of the sample whose ARPES data is presented in Fig.2 b), and in d) the same for the ARPES data shown in Fig.2 c) or Fig.1 b).
